title: Forms
narrativeIntro: The final region requires precision. Input, validation, error handling — every piece of data that enters the system must be verified and shaped. The builders were careful here. Forms are how users speak to applications, and that conversation must be handled with care.
narrativeOutro: Forms are the most human part of any application. They're where real people type real things and expect the system to understand. You handle that now — validation, feedback, guidance. The Drift's final region begins to glow.
phmConnectionNote: Ryland needs precise input mechanisms for critical operations. You learn Angular forms.
conceptsPreview: Reactive forms, FormGroup, FormControl, validators, valueChanges, and FormBuilder

challenges.title: Form State
challenges.narrativeHook: A form is touched, dirty, and invalid. The template must respond to all three.
challenges.instructions:
A reactive form has one `Validators.required` control. The user has typed into it (dirty, touched) but then deleted the text (value is now `''`).

Predict the form's state properties.
challenges.predictPrompt: What are form.valid, form.dirty, and form.touched?
predictChoices: valid: false, dirty: true, touched: true
valid: false, dirty: false, touched: true
valid: true, dirty: true, touched: true
valid: false, dirty: true, touched: false
challenges.predictAnswer: valid: false, dirty: true, touched: true

explainGate.prompt: What is the difference between a form being 'dirty' and being 'touched'?

hints: valid: Validators.required fails for empty string → false.
dirty: the user changed the value (typed then deleted) → true.
touched: the user focused and then blurred (left) the field → true.

challenges.title: Build a Form
challenges.narrativeHook: A crew registration form. Two fields, both required. Build the reactive form group.
challenges.instructions:
Complete the `RegistrationComponent`:

- Build a `FormGroup` with two `FormControl`s:
  - `callsign` — required, minLength 3
  - `rank` — required
- `isValid` getter returns `this.form.valid`
- `getValues()` returns `this.form.value`

files.name: registration.component.ts

tests.description: form should be invalid when both fields are empty
tests.testCode: const c = new RegistrationComponent(); if (c.isValid) throw new Error('form should be invalid when fields are empty');

tests.description: form should be invalid when callsign is too short
tests.testCode: const c = new RegistrationComponent(); c.form.get('callsign').value = 'ab'; c.form.get('rank').value = 'commander'; if (c.isValid) throw new Error('form should be invalid when callsign < 3 chars');

tests.description: form should be valid when both fields are filled correctly
tests.testCode: const c = new RegistrationComponent(); c.form.get('callsign').value = 'alpha'; c.form.get('rank').value = 'commander'; if (!c.isValid) throw new Error('form should be valid with callsign=alpha and rank=commander');

tests.description: getValues() should return both field values
tests.testCode: const c = new RegistrationComponent(); c.form.get('callsign').value = 'gamma'; c.form.get('rank').value = 'pilot'; const v = c.getValues(); if (v.callsign !== 'gamma' || v.rank !== 'pilot') throw new Error(`getValues() returned ${JSON.stringify(v)}`);

hints: Create the form in the constructor: this.form = new FormGroup({ callsign: new FormControl('', Validators.required, Validators.minLength(3)), rank: new FormControl('', Validators.required) });
Or use the field initializer syntax: form = new FormGroup({ ... });

explainGate.prompt: In real Angular codebases, most developers use FormBuilder instead of constructing FormGroup and FormControl by hand. How does `this.fb.group({ callsign: ['', [Validators.required, Validators.minLength(3)]], rank: ['', Validators.required] })` compare to what you just built? What does FormBuilder add?

challenges.title: Validator Short-Circuit
challenges.narrativeHook: The form validates but always says valid. The validator runs — it just never rejects.
challenges.instructions:
The `passwordValidator` should reject passwords shorter than 8 characters. It always returns `null` (valid).

There is exactly one bug in the validator function. Fix it.

files.name: login.component.ts

tests.description: passwordValidator should return error for password shorter than 8 chars
tests.testCode: const err = passwordValidator({ value: 'abc' }); if (!err || !err.tooShort) throw new Error('passwordValidator should return { tooShort: true } for short passwords');

tests.description: passwordValidator should return null for password of 8+ chars
tests.testCode: const err = passwordValidator({ value: 'longpass1' }); if (err !== null) throw new Error(`passwordValidator should return null for valid password, got ${JSON.stringify(err)}`);

tests.description: LoginComponent.passwordError should return message for short password
tests.testCode: const c = new LoginComponent(); c.passwordControl.value = 'short'; if (c.passwordError !== 'Password too short') throw new Error(`passwordError should be 'Password too short', got '${c.passwordError}'`);

hints: The condition is inverted: value.length > 8 returns an error only for LONG passwords.
A password shorter than 8 characters should be rejected: change > to <

challenges.title: Submit Guard
challenges.narrativeHook: The form submits only when valid. Invalid submissions are blocked and explained.
challenges.instructions:
Complete the `CommandFormComponent`:

- `form` has two fields: `target` (required, minLength 2) and `power` (required)
- `submit()` does nothing if `form.invalid`, otherwise sets `submitted = true` and stores `form.value` in `lastSubmission`
- `canSubmit` getter returns `form.valid`

files.name: command-form.component.ts

tests.description: submit() should not set submitted when form is invalid
tests.testCode: const c = new CommandFormComponent(); c.submit(); if (c.submitted) throw new Error('submit() should not set submitted when form is invalid');

tests.description: submit() should set submitted when form is valid
tests.testCode: const c = new CommandFormComponent(); c.form.get('target').value = 'beacon-7'; c.form.get('power').value = '80'; c.submit(); if (!c.submitted) throw new Error('submit() should set submitted when form is valid');

tests.description: submit() should store form values in lastSubmission
tests.testCode: const c = new CommandFormComponent(); c.form.get('target').value = 'relay'; c.form.get('power').value = '50'; c.submit(); if (!c.lastSubmission || c.lastSubmission.target !== 'relay') throw new Error(`lastSubmission.target should be 'relay', got ${JSON.stringify(c.lastSubmission)}`);

const c = new CommandFormComponent(); if (c.canSubmit) throw new Error('canSubmit should be false when form is empty'); c.form.get('target').value = 'ab'; c.form.get('power').value = '100'; if (!c.canSubmit) throw new Error('canSubmit should be true when form is valid');

hints: form = new FormGroup({ target: new FormControl('', Validators.required, Validators.minLength(2)), power: new FormControl('', Validators.required) });
submit(): if (this.form.invalid) return; this.submitted = true; this.lastSubmission = this.form.value;

challenges.title: Watching the Stream
challenges.narrativeHook: The form is wired and listening. Every keystroke becomes data. What arrives on the other end of this stream?
challenges.instructions:
An Angular reactive form has a `valueChanges` Observable on every control. It emits the control's current value each time the user types.

A component subscribes to the `callsign` field's `valueChanges` in `ngOnInit`. The user types one character at a time: `'a'`, then `'l'`, then `'p'`.

Predict what gets logged to the console.
challenges.predictPrompt: What does the console log after the user types 'a', then 'l', then 'p'?
predictChoices:
current: alp
(once — the final value only)

current: a
current: al
current: alp

current: ["a", "al", "alp"]
(all values collected in an array)
Nothing — valueChanges only fires on form submit
challenges.predictAnswer:
current: a
current: al
current: alp

explainGate.prompt: valueChanges is an Observable. How would you use pipe(debounceTime(300)) with it — and why would you want that when wiring a search input to an API call?

hints: valueChanges emits every time the control's value changes — once per keystroke, not once per form submit.
An Observable subscriber is called for each emission. Three keystrokes = three separate emissions.
The value emitted is the control's current value at the moment of each change: first 'a', then 'al', then 'alp'.
